subject: ap-calculus-ab
title: Swimming Particle — Direction Change, Acceleration & Distance
stimulus: Stephen swims back and forth along a straight path in a 50-meter-long pool for 90 seconds. Stephen's velocity is modeled by $v(t) = 2.38e^{-0.02t}\sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{56}\right)$, where $t$ is measured in seconds and $v(t)$ is measured in meters per second.

parts.prompt: Find all times $t$ in the interval $0 < t < 90$ at which Stephen changes direction. Give a reason for your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Must consider $v(t) = 0$ or the sign of $v(t)$ for first point. The only zero in $(0, 90)$ is $t = 56$ (since $\sin(\pi t/56) = 0 \Rightarrow t = 56$ in this interval). An answer of $t = 56$ with no supporting work earns no points.

scoring_points.description: Considers sign of $v(t)$: sets $v(t) = 0$ and finds $t = 56$ in the interval $0 < t < 90$
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: considers $v(t) = 0$ or the sign of $v(t)$; earns only if the response includes the analysis (bare answer of $t=56$ earns nothing).

sample_responses.response: Stephen changes direction when $v(t) = 0$. For $0 < t < 90$: $v(t) = 0 \Rightarrow \sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{56}\right) = 0 \Rightarrow t = 56$.
sample_responses.commentary: $2.38e^{-0.02t} > 0$ for all $t$, so $v(t) = 0$ only when $\sin(\pi t/56) = 0$, i.e., $\pi t/56 = \pi$, giving $t = 56$ in the interval.
An unsupported answer of $t = 56$ with no analysis of $v(t) = 0$ or sign change earns neither point.

scoring_points.description: Stephen changes direction at $t = 56$ seconds because his velocity changes sign at that time

required_elements: Stephen changes direction at $t = 56$ seconds because his velocity changes sign at that time

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: states $t = 56$ is the answer with reason that velocity changes sign there. Must earn P1 first.

sample_responses.response: Stephen changes direction at $t = 56$ seconds because his velocity changes sign from positive to negative at that time.
sample_responses.commentary: For $0 < t < 56$: $v(t) > 0$ (moving in one direction); for $56 < t < 90$: $v(t) < 0$ (moving in the opposite direction); the sign change confirms direction reversal.

sample_responses.response: Stephen changes direction at $t = 56$ seconds because his acceleration equals zero there.
sample_responses.commentary: Direction change is indicated by a sign change in velocity, not by acceleration being zero; $a(56) = 0$ is neither necessary nor sufficient for direction change.

parts.prompt: Find Stephen's acceleration at time $t = 60$ seconds. Show the setup for your calculations, and indicate units of measure. Is Stephen speeding up or slowing down at time $t = 60$ seconds? Give a reason for your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Must explicitly show $v'(60) = a(60)$ connection for setup point. Units are required. For the speed question: $v(60) < 0$ and $a(60) < 0$ (same sign) means speeding up. A response does not need to find the exact value of $v(60)$; stating $v(60) < 0$ (implied from $t = 60 > 56$) is sufficient.

alternatives.correct_example: $a(60) = v'(60) \approx -0.036$ meters per second per second

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: must explicitly show $v'(60) = a(60)$ or $v(t) = a(t)$; minimum accepted is $v'(60) = -0.036$. Simply writing $a(60) = -0.036$ without the connection to $v'$ does not earn this point.

sample_responses.response: $a(60) = v'(60) \approx -0.036$ meters per second per second.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly links acceleration to the derivative of velocity and computes $v'(60)$ numerically; connection $a=v'$ is explicit.

sample_responses.response: $a(60) \approx -0.036$ meters per second per second (no setup shown).
sample_responses.commentary: The connection $a(60) = v'(60)$ must be shown explicitly; stating only the numerical value without the derivative setup does not earn this point.

scoring_points.description: Correct units: meters per second per second (or m/s$^2$)

required_elements: Correct units: meters per second per second (or m/s$^2$)

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: units of meters per second per second (m/s² or m/sec²) stated; must have a declared value for $a(60)$ to be eligible.

sample_responses.response: Stephen's acceleration at $t = 60$ seconds is $-0.036$ meters per second per second.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceleration has dimensions of $\frac{\text{meters/second}}{\text{second}}$ = meters per second per second; this is correct.

sample_responses.response: Stephen's acceleration at $t = 60$ seconds is $-0.036$ meters per second.
sample_responses.commentary: Meters per second is the unit of velocity, not acceleration; acceleration must be expressed as meters per second per second (or m/s²).

scoring_points.description: Stephen is speeding up at $t = 60$ seconds because $v(60) \approx -0.160 < 0$ and $a(60) \approx -0.036 < 0$ have the same sign

required_elements: Stephen is speeding up at $t = 60$ seconds because $v(60) \approx -0.160 < 0$

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: conclusion consistent with a negative $v(60)$ and the declared $a(60)$; may state $v(60)$ and $a(60)$ have the same sign without computing $v(60)$ explicitly.

sample_responses.response: Stephen is speeding up at $t = 60$ seconds because $v(60) \approx -0.160 < 0$ and $a(60) \approx -0.036 < 0$ have the same sign.
sample_responses.commentary: An object speeds up when velocity and acceleration have the same sign (both pulling in the same direction); both are negative here, confirming Stephen is speeding up.

sample_responses.response: Stephen is slowing down at $t = 60$ seconds because his acceleration is negative.
sample_responses.commentary: Negative acceleration alone does not imply slowing down; since velocity is also negative at $t = 60$, the speed $|v|$ is increasing — Stephen is speeding up.

parts.prompt: Find the distance between Stephen's position at time $t = 20$ seconds and his position at time $t = 80$ seconds. Show the setup for your calculations.
parts.scoring_notes: The integral $\int_{20}^{80} v(t)\, dt$ gives displacement. The distance between positions is the absolute value. Answer $\approx 23.384$ meters. Only $\int_{20}^{80} v(t)\, dt$ earns the integral point (not $\int_{20}^{80} |v(t)|\, dt$ which would give total distance).

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: $\int_{20}^{80} v(t)\,dt$ (signed integral giving displacement); note $\int_{20}^{80}|v(t)|\,dt$ would give total distance, which is a different quantity.

sample_responses.commentary: Distance between two positions equals the absolute value of displacement, which is $|\int_{20}^{80} v(t)\,dt|$; the unsigned integral gives the signed displacement.

sample_responses.response: Distance $= \displaystyle\int_{20}^{80} |v(t)|\,dt$ (total path length instead of distance between positions).
sample_responses.commentary: $\int_{20}^{80}|v(t)|\,dt$ gives the total path length, not the distance between the two positions; the problem asks for distance between positions, which uses the (unsigned) displacement integral.

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: correct answer $23.384$ (or $23.383$) meters; earned regardless of whether the setup point was earned.

sample_responses.commentary: Calculator evaluation gives $\int_{20}^{80} v(t)\,dt \approx 23.384$; since the value is positive, the absolute value does not change it.
Three decimal places are required; $23.38$ is insufficiently precise.

parts.prompt: Find the total distance Stephen swims over the time interval $0 \le t \le 90$ seconds. Show the setup for your calculations.
parts.scoring_notes: Total distance requires integrating $|v(t)|$ or splitting at $t = 56$. Answer $\approx 62.164$ meters. The answer point is earned for $62.164$ regardless of whether the setup point was earned.

alternatives.correct_example: $\int_0^{90} |v(t)|\, dt$ or equivalent split: $\int_0^{56} v(t)\, dt - \int_{56}^{90} v(t)\, dt$

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: $\int_0^{90}|v(t)|\,dt$ or equivalent split at $t=56$: $\int_0^{56} v(t)\,dt - \int_{56}^{90} v(t)\,dt$.

sample_responses.commentary: Total distance (arc length along the number line) is always $\int|v(t)|\,dt$; the absolute value accounts for the direction reversal at $t = 56$.

sample_responses.response: Total distance $= \displaystyle\int_0^{90} v(t)\,dt$ (signed integral, no absolute value).
sample_responses.commentary: The signed integral $\int_0^{90} v(t)\,dt$ gives net displacement (which could cancel if Stephen swims back), not total distance traveled; absolute value or splitting at $t=56$ is required.

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: correct answer $62.164$ meters; earned regardless of whether the setup point was earned.

sample_responses.commentary: Calculator evaluation of the total distance integral gives $62.164$ meters to three decimal places.
Must report three decimal places; $62.2$ is insufficiently precise and does not earn the answer point.
